radiology_order.exam: US ABD LIMIT, SINGLE ORGAN PORT

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP complication: hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible expansion or rupture, biloma, hepatic abscess, biliary perforation/leak, cholangitis, or mesenteric ischemia recurrence given prior celiac/SMA stents
ensemble.information_gap: The outside CT described pneumobilia and a possible subcapsular hepatic hematoma with high-density fluid, but this institution has no cross-sectional imaging of its own to characterize the extent, stability, or evolution of these findings — specifically whether the hematoma has expanded, ruptured, or become infected, and whether there is a biliary leak or abscess forming
ensemble.expected_finding: Characterization of the right hepatic subcapsular collection (size, density, rim enhancement suggesting abscess vs. simple hematoma), evidence of active bleeding or contrast extravasation, pneumobilia extent, free intraperitoneal fluid or air suggesting biliary perforation, and assessment of celiac/SMA stent patency given history of mesenteric ischemia
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient was transferred with an outside CT report of hepatic subcapsular hematoma and pneumobilia post-ERCP, but no imaging has been performed at this institution; with worsening fever (103.4→100.1 on arrival), leukocytosis (23k), metabolic acidosis (bicarb 16, AG 21), tachycardia, and RUQ guarding, a contrast-enhanced CT is essential to directly characterize the hepatic lesion, identify any biliary complication, and assess vascular patency.
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'developed a fever with Tm 103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening throughout the day'
Vitals: '100.1 PO 95/61 R Sitting 119 18 97 2L' — hypotension and tachycardia on arrival
Physical exam: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL', 'Bicarbonate: 16.0 mEq/L', 'Anion Gap: 21.0 mEq/L' — leukocytosis with metabolic acidosis
History: 'Mesenteric ischemia s/p celiac artery stent placement...s/p SMA stent placement' — vascular history requiring assessment
History: 'transferred for evaluation for complications following ERCP'
Imaging already resulted: '(none — only the baseline workup below was available)' — no imaging performed at this institution yet
ensemble.reasoning: This patient presents with post-ERCP fever, leukocytosis, metabolic acidosis, hypotension, tachycardia, and RUQ guarding after transfer with an outside report of hepatic subcapsular hematoma and pneumobilia — but I have no imaging of my own. The critical uncertainty is whether the hepatic collection has expanded, become infected (abscess), or whether there is an ongoing biliary leak or perforation that would require urgent intervention; contrast-enhanced CT will characterize the hepatic lesion, identify free fluid or air, and also allow assessment of the celiac/SMA stents given his history of mesenteric ischemia and current hemodynamic instability.

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient presents with post-ERCP fever, leukocytosis, metabolic acidosis, hypotension, tachycardia, and RUQ guarding after transfer with an outside report of hepatic subcapsular hematoma and pneumobilia — but I have no imaging of my own. The critical uncertainty is whether the hepatic collection has expanded, become infected (abscess), or whether there is an ongoing biliary leak or perforation that would require urgent intervention; contrast-enhanced CT will characterize the hepatic lesion, identify free fluid or air, and also allow assessment of the celiac/SMA stents given his history of mesenteric ischemia and current hemodynamic instability.

vindication.actual_finding: CT demonstrated a 4.0 x 1.3 cm subcapsular collection of very high-density material and gas in hepatic segments V/VIII consistent with a hematoma with superimposed gas (suggesting possible infection or biliary communication), extensive pneumobilia in the common bile duct and gallbladder, interval removal of the CBD stent, celiac and SMA stents present with noted prior celiac stent thrombus, and no free intraperitoneal fluid or air.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The scan directly characterized the subcapsular hepatic collection (size, density, and presence of gas suggesting possible infected hematoma or biliary fistula), confirmed pneumobilia extent, assessed stent patency, and excluded free intraperitoneal fluid or active contrast extravasation, addressing nearly all components of the physician's expected findings.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP biliary complication: bile duct injury, biloma, or biliary leak; hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible biliary communication; cholangitis; hepatic abscess formation; or vascular injury to hepatic arterial supply compromising biliary tree
ensemble.information_gap: The CT shows a subcapsular hepatic collection (gas + high-density fluid at segment V/VIII) and extensive pneumobilia after ERCP stent removal, but cannot characterize whether there is an active biliary leak, bile duct disruption, or injury to the intrahepatic biliary radicles that would explain the collection and the patient's septic picture
ensemble.expected_finding: MRCP is expected to delineate the intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary anatomy in detail — specifically to identify a biliary leak or ductal disruption communicating with the subcapsular collection, residual filling defects (stones), stricture, or biliary-vascular fistula that CT contrast could not resolve
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The CT has identified a post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular gas-and-fluid collection with extensive pneumobilia but cannot characterize biliary ductal integrity; MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard for mapping biliary anatomy and detecting leaks or ductal injuries that would guide the next intervention (repeat ERCP, percutaneous drainage, or surgery).
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'Based on severity of illness and recent ERCP he was transferred for evaluation for complications following ERCP'
Prior imaging: 'very high density material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm'

Prior imaging: 'significant pneumobilia in the common bile duct extending into the left intrahepatic bile duct and a trace amount extending into the right intrahepatic bile duct... gas within the cystic duct and nearly completely filling the gallbladder'
History: 'CBD stricture s/p CBD stent placement ___, s/p ERCP ___ for removal of stents'
History: 'ERCP with stent removal and gallstone removal ___ (yesterday)'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL'; 'Lactate: 0.8 mmol/L'; temperature 100.1 on admission after Tm 103.4 at OSH
Physical exam: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient is septic (WBC 23k, fever to 103.4, tachycardia) one day after ERCP with stent and stone removal, and CT has revealed a subcapsular hepatic collection containing gas and high-density fluid at segment V/VIII alongside extensive pneumobilia — a pattern highly concerning for a post-ERCP biliary injury or leak rather than simple post-procedural pneumobilia. The CT cannot define whether the intrahepatic bile ducts are disrupted or whether there is a biliary-subcapsular communication; MRCP is ordered to map the biliary tree in detail, identify any ductal discontinuity or leak, and guide whether repeat ERCP, percutaneous drainage, or surgical intervention is needed.

representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The CT has identified a post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular gas-and-fluid collection with extensive pneumobilia but cannot characterize biliary ductal integrity; MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard for mapping biliary anatomy and detecting leaks or ductal injuries that would guide the next intervention (repeat ERCP, percutaneous drainage, or surgery).

vindication.actual_finding: MRCP demonstrated subcapsular hepatic fluid collections at segment VIII, pneumobilia in the CBD/gallbladder/left intrahepatic ducts, an aberrant posterior right intrahepatic bile duct inserting on the left system, no biliary ductal dilatation, no peribiliary enhancement, and no evidence of active biliary leak, ductal disruption, filling defects, stricture, or biliary-vascular fistula.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The MRCP failed to identify the expected biliary leak, ductal disruption communicating with the subcapsular collection, residual stones, or biliary-vascular fistula, leaving the etiology of the collection and septic picture unresolved by this modality.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (US ABD LIMIT, SINGLE ORGAN PORT)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible active bleeding or expansion; biloma; hepatic abscess; biliary complication (bile leak, biliary obstruction); cholangitis
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the known hepatic subcapsular collection (segment V/VIII on CT, segment VIII on MRCP) is stable, expanding, or showing signs of active hemorrhage — and whether there is new or evolving free fluid or biliary pathology — without additional radiation exposure and at the bedside given the patient's clinical deterioration (fever to 103.4, WBC 23k, worsening pain, hemodynamic instability).
ensemble.expected_finding: Interval change in size of the hepatic subcapsular hematoma/collection (growth suggesting active bleeding or superinfection), new perihepatic free fluid suggesting bile leak or hemoperitoneum, or gallbladder wall changes suggesting cholecystitis as a contributing source.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has a known hepatic subcapsular collection identified on both CT and MRCP following ERCP, is now clinically deteriorating with fever, leukocytosis to 23k, tachycardia, and worsening RUQ pain, making bedside ultrasound a rational, radiation-free, rapid tool to assess for interval expansion of the collection or new free fluid before deciding on invasive intervention.
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'the morning of transfer he developed a fever with Tm 103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening throughout the day'
Physical examination: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'
Prior imaging 1 (CT): 'very high density material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm'

Prior imaging 2 (MRCP): 'approximately 1.5 x 1.3 cm T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, nonenhancing fluid collection the subcapsular aspect of segment VIII' with 'additional, smaller T2 hyperintense foci'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL', 'Hemoglobin: 9.7 g/dL', 'Hematocrit: 30.0 %'
Vitals: '100.1 PO 95 / 61 R Sitting 119 18 97 2L' — hypotension and tachycardia on admission
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a known post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular hematoma/collection that was characterized on CT and MRCP, but he is now clinically deteriorating with worsening fever, leukocytosis, hypotension, tachycardia, and severe RUQ pain with guarding — raising concern that the collection is expanding, becoming infected (hepatic abscess), or that a bile leak is developing. A limited bedside ultrasound of the right upper quadrant is a rapid, radiation-free way to assess interval change in the collection size, detect new perihepatic free fluid, and evaluate the gallbladder, directly informing the urgency and nature of any procedural intervention (e.g., IR drainage vs. emergent surgery).

vindication.actual_finding: Technically limited bedside RUQ ultrasound showed only trace perihepatic/subhepatic fluid, pneumobilia consistent with prior CT, a contracted gallbladder with a catheter in place, and no focal liver lesion identified on this limited exam.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (US ABD LIMIT, SINGLE ORGAN)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible expansion or superinfection; biloma or hepatic abscess formation; biliary leak; cholangitis; percutaneous drain/catheter position assessment in gallbladder
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the known hepatic subcapsular fluid collection (segment V/VIII) has changed in size, developed new characteristics suggesting active bleeding or abscess formation, and whether the gallbladder catheter seen on prior ultrasound is appropriately positioned and draining — information that bedside ultrasound can provide rapidly without radiation or contrast.
ensemble.expected_finding: Interval change in the subcapsular hepatic fluid collection (enlargement suggesting active hemorrhage, or new internal echogenicity/debris suggesting superinfection/abscess), assessment of perihepatic free fluid, and confirmation of gallbladder catheter position and any residual gallbladder distension or pericholecystic fluid.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has a known hepatic subcapsular hematoma on prior CT/MRCP, is now febrile to 103.3°F with WBC 23K, worsening RUQ pain, and guarding — a repeat bedside ultrasound is a rational, rapid, radiation-free method to assess for interval progression of the collection or new complications at the bedside in a critically ill patient.
grounding: History: 'CT scan performed which revealed pneumobilia, mild collection of gas and high density fluid at periphery of right hepatic lobe, suggesting subcapsular hematoma'
History: 'The morning of transfer he developed a fever with Tm 103.4, WBCs of 18k, and with worsening abdominal pain worsening throughout the day'
Physical examination: 'Abdomen is diffusely tender though most notably in RUQ, there is guarding but no rebound'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 23.0 K/uL' and 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 20.7 K/uL'
Prior imaging 1 (CT): 'very high density material and gas apparently within the subcapsular aspect of segment V/VIII spanning in total approximately 4.0 x 1.3 cm'
Prior imaging 2 (MRCP): 'approximately 1.5 x 1.3 cm T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, nonenhancing fluid collection the subcapsular aspect of segment VIII'

ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a known post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular hematoma that was characterized on CT and MRCP, but he has now deteriorated with high fever, leukocytosis of 23K, hypotension, tachycardia, and worsening RUQ pain with guarding — raising concern that the collection has expanded (active hemorrhage) or become infected (hepatic abscess/biloma). A bedside ultrasound is the fastest, safest way to reassess the collection's size and echogenicity, evaluate for new perihepatic free fluid, and confirm the gallbladder catheter position, all of which would directly guide the next intervention (IR drainage vs. surgical vs. continued conservative management).

steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Post-ERCP complication: hepatic subcapsular hematoma expansion, biloma, hepatic abscess, biliary leak, or vascular injury (hepatic artery/portal vein); also mesenteric ischemia given known celiac/SMA stents with possible in-stent stenosis/thrombosis; ascending cholangitis; intraperitoneal hemorrhage
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the known subcapsular hepatic collection (segment V/VIII) has expanded, developed into an abscess, or is associated with a biliary leak or active hemorrhage — and whether there is a new or evolving intra-abdominal process (e.g., vascular compromise, free perforation, or peritonitis) explaining the clinical deterioration (fever to 103.4, WBC 23k, worsening RUQ pain, altered mental status, tachycardia, hypotension) since the prior MRCP/CT
ensemble.expected_finding: Interval change in the hepatic subcapsular collection (enlargement, new rim enhancement suggesting abscess formation, or active extravasation of contrast suggesting hemorrhage); alternatively, new free fluid, pneumoperitoneum, bowel wall ischemia, or biliary leak with perihepatic fluid collection
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has clinically deteriorated since prior imaging (rising WBC from 18k to 23k, persistent high fever, worsening RUQ pain, altered mental status, tachycardia, and hypotension) with a known post-ERCP hepatic subcapsular collection and complex vascular history, making interval CT with IV contrast the appropriate next step to reassess the collection and exclude new or evolving intra-abdominal catastrophe.

ensemble.reasoning: This patient has clinically worsened since the prior imaging (MRCP and outside CT) with escalating leukocytosis (18k → 23k), persistent high fever, RUQ guarding, hypotension, and encephalopathy following ERCP — all pointing to a serious post-procedural complication. The known hepatic subcapsular collection (segment V/VIII, with gas and high-density fluid) could have expanded, become infected (hepatic abscess), or be associated with active hemorrhage or biliary leak; additionally, his history of celiac/SMA stents with known celiac thrombus raises concern for mesenteric ischemia as a concurrent or contributing process. A repeat CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast is ordered to characterize interval change in the hepatic collection, identify any new free fluid or pneumoperitoneum, assess bowel wall enhancement for ischemia, and guide the next intervention (drainage, IR, or surgery).

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has clinically worsened since the prior imaging (MRCP and outside CT) with escalating leukocytosis (18k → 23k), persistent high fever, RUQ guarding, hypotension, and encephalopathy following ERCP — all pointing to a serious post-procedural complication. The known hepatic subcapsular collection (segment V/VIII, with gas and high-density fluid) could have expanded, become infected (hepatic abscess), or be associated with active hemorrhage or biliary leak; additionally, his history of celiac/SMA stents with known celiac thrombus raises concern for mesenteric ischemia as a concurrent or contributing process. A repeat CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast is ordered to characterize interval change in the hepatic collection, identify any new free fluid or pneumoperitoneum, assess bowel wall enhancement for ischemia, and guide the next intervention (drainage, IR, or surgery).

vindication.actual_finding: The known subcapsular hepatic collections (segment 8) are stable in size without significant rim enhancement or active extravasation, but new findings include a small air bubble within the largest collection, new mild-volume ascites, and new pancolitis consistent with C. difficile infection — the latter representing a newly identified intra-abdominal process explaining clinical deterioration.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The expectation of interval change in the hepatic collection was partially met (new air bubble, though stable size), and the broader expectation of a new or evolving intra-abdominal process explaining clinical deterioration was confirmed by the discovery of new pancolitis (C. difficile) and new ascites, even though abscess formation, active hemorrhage, or biliary leak were not demonstrated.

ensemble.information_gap: Whether the known subcapsular hepatic fluid collections have enlarged, become infected (abscess), or whether new complications (biliary leak, worsening ascites, colitis progression, vascular compromise) have developed since the most recent prior CT, given the patient's clinical deterioration with rising WBC to 23k, persistent fever, worsening abdominal pain, and metabolic acidosis (bicarb 16, AG 21)
ensemble.expected_finding: Interval enlargement or new rim-enhancement of the subcapsular hepatic fluid collections suggesting abscess formation, new or worsening perihepatic/intraperitoneal free fluid suggesting biliary leak or hemorrhage, worsening colitis, or evidence of vascular compromise at the celiac/SMA stents
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has clinically deteriorated since the last CT (WBC 23k up from 18k, persistent high fever, worsening pain, metabolic acidosis with AG 21) and the prior CT already identified subcapsular hepatic collections and pancolitis, making interval reassessment with contrast CT the appropriate next step to guide intervention decisions.

vindication.actual_finding: The subcapsular hepatic fluid collections are unchanged or slightly smaller, with no rim-enhancement, no new perihepatic free fluid, no biliary leak, no pneumatosis or bowel obstruction, stable mild ascites, and patent SMA stents with no new vascular compromise, though persistent sigmoid/rectal wall thickening remains.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The physician expected interval enlargement, abscess formation, biliary leak, hemorrhage, or vascular compromise to explain clinical deterioration, but the CT showed stable or improved hepatic collections, stable ascites, and no new complications.
